[Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") is the largest and the [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") of the [People's Republic of Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh").[[1]](#cite_note-aljazeera:1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-reuters:1-2) According to the 2022 census, [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") had a population of about 150 million [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim"), or 91.04%[[3]](#cite_note-3) of its total population of 165 million.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4) Muslims of Bangladesh are predominant native [Bengali Muslims](/wiki/Bengali_Muslim "Bengali Muslim"). The majority of [Bangladeshis](/wiki/Bangladeshis "Bangladeshis") are [Sunni](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam"), and follow the [Hanafi](/wiki/Hanafi "Hanafi") school of thought and *[fiqh](/wiki/Fiqh "Fiqh")*. Bangladesh is a *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* secular country.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-bbs-6)

The [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal") region was a supreme power of the medieval Islamic East.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In the late 7th century, Muslims from Arabia established commercial as well as religious connection within the Bengal region before the conquest, mainly through the coastal regions as traders and primarily via the ports of [Chittagong](/wiki/Chittagong "Chittagong").[[8]](#cite_note-kumar-8) In the early 13th century, [Muhammad bin Bakhtiyar Khalji](/wiki/Muhammad_bin_Bakhtiyar_Khalji "Muhammad bin Bakhtiyar Khalji") conquered Western and part of Northern Bengal and established the first Muslim kingdom in Bengal.[[9]](#cite_note-9) During the 13th century, Sufi missionaries, mystics and saints began to preach Islam in villages.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The Islamic [Bengal Sultanate](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate") was founded by [Shamsuddin Ilyas Shah](/wiki/Shamsuddin_Ilyas_Shah "Shamsuddin Ilyas Shah") who united Bengal on an ethno-linguistic platform. Bengal reached in her golden age during Bengal Sultanate's prosperous ruling period. Subsequently, Bengal viceroy [Muhammad Azam Shah](/wiki/Muhammad_Azam_Shah "Muhammad Azam Shah") assumed the imperial throne. Mughal Bengal became increasingly independent under the [Nawabs of Bengal](/wiki/Nawabs_of_Bengal "Nawabs of Bengal") in the 18th century.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early explorers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early explorers")]

One of the earliest mosques in South Asia is under excavation in northern Bangladesh, indicating the presence of Muslims in the area around the lifetime of Muhammad.[[14]](#cite_note-14) By the 9th century, Muslim merchants had increased trade with Bengali seaports, introducing Islam to the region. Coins from the Abbasid era have been discovered across Bengal, further evidencing this connection. Additional evidence of these early Arab traders includes inscriptions found near the [Meghna River](/wiki/Meghna_River "Meghna River") close to [Sandwip](/wiki/Sandwip "Sandwip") on the Bay of Bengal. Arab writers of the time also mentioned the kingdoms of Samrup and Rumi, the latter being identified with the empire of [Dharmapal](/wiki/Dharmapal "Dharmapal") of the [Pala Empire](/wiki/Pala_Dynasty "Pala Dynasty").

In the 10th century, Samatata, located in southeastern Bengal, was home to a diverse population with various religious backgrounds. During this period, the Arab geographer [Al-Masudi](/wiki/Al-Masudi "Al-Masudi") visited the region and documented a thriving Muslim community in his work *[The Meadows of Gold](/wiki/The_Meadows_of_Gold "The Meadows of Gold").*[[15]](#cite_note-15) Islam first appeared in Bengal during [Pala dynasty's](/wiki/Pala_dynasty "Pala dynasty") rule, as a result of increased trade between Bengal and the Arab [Abbasid Caliphate](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate"), facilitating the presence of Arab traders along the coast of Bengal which includes [Port of Chittagong](/wiki/Port_of_Chittagong "Port of Chittagong").[[8]](#cite_note-kumar-8)

In addition to trade, Islam was also being introduced to the people of Bengal through the migration of Sufi missionaries prior to conquests. The earliest known Sufi missionaries were Syed Shah Surkhul Antia and his students, most notably [Shah Sultan Rumi](/wiki/Shah_Sultan_Rumi "Shah Sultan Rumi"), in the 11th century. Rumi settled in present-day [Netrokona, Mymensingh](/wiki/Netrokona_District "Netrokona District") where he influenced the local ruler and population to embrace Islam.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The first [Muslim conquest of Bengal](/wiki/Ghurid_invasion_of_Bengal "Ghurid invasion of Bengal") was undertaken by the forces of General [Bakhtiyar Khilji](/wiki/Bakhtiyar_Khilji "Bakhtiyar Khilji") in the thirteenth century. This opened the doors for Muslim influence in the region for hundreds of years up until the present-day.[[17]](#cite_note-histglo-17) Many of the people of Bengal began accepting Islam through the influx of missionaries following this conquest. [Sultan Balkhi](/wiki/Sultan_Balkhi "Sultan Balkhi") and [Shah Makhdum Rupos](/wiki/Shah_Makhdum_Rupos "Shah Makhdum Rupos") settled in the present-day [Rajshahi Division](/wiki/Rajshahi_Division "Rajshahi Division") in northern Bengal, preaching to the communities there. Numerous small sultanates emerged in the region. During the reign of the [Sultan of Lakhnauti](/wiki/Lakhnauti_Sultanate "Lakhnauti Sultanate") [Shamsuddin Firuz Shah](/wiki/Shamsuddin_Firuz_Shah "Shamsuddin Firuz Shah"), much of present-day [Satgaon](/wiki/Satgaon "Satgaon"), [Sonargaon](/wiki/Sonargaon "Sonargaon") and [Mymensingh](/wiki/Greater_Mymensingh "Greater Mymensingh") came under Muslim dominion. A community of 13 Muslim families headed by [Burhanuddin](/wiki/Ghazi_Burhanuddin "Ghazi Burhanuddin") resided in the northeastern city of [Srihatta (Sylhet)](/wiki/Sylhet "Sylhet"), claiming their descendants to have arrived from [Chittagong](/wiki/Chittagong "Chittagong").[[18]](#cite_note-18) [Srihatta](/wiki/Sylhet "Sylhet") (Sylhet) was ruled by an oppressive king called [Gour Govinda](/wiki/Gour_Govinda "Gour Govinda"). After being informed of Raja Gour Govinda's oppressive regime in Sylhet, Firuz Shah sent numerous forces led by his nephew [Sikandar Khan Ghazi](/wiki/Sikandar_Khan_Ghazi "Sikandar Khan Ghazi") and subsequently his military commander-in-chief [Syed Nasiruddin](/wiki/Syed_Nasiruddin "Syed Nasiruddin") to [conquer Sylhet](/wiki/Conquest_of_Sylhet "Conquest of Sylhet"). By 1303, over three hundred Sufi preachers led by [Shah Jalal](/wiki/Shah_Jalal "Shah Jalal") aided the conquest and confirmed a victory. Following the conquest, Jalal disseminated his followers across different parts of Bengal to spread Islam. Jalal is now a household name among Muslims in Bangladesh.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Sultanate of Bengal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Sultanate of Bengal")]

Main articles: [History of Bengal](/wiki/History_of_Bengal "History of Bengal"), [History of Bangladesh](/wiki/History_of_Bangladesh "History of Bangladesh"), [History of West Bengal](/wiki/History_of_West_Bengal "History of West Bengal"), and [Bengal Sultanate](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate")

The establishment of a single united [Bengal Sultanate](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate") in 1352 by [Shamsuddin Ilyas Shah](/wiki/Shamsuddin_Ilyas_Shah "Shamsuddin Ilyas Shah") finally gave rise to a *"Bengali"* socio-linguistic and cultural identity. The [Ilyas Shahi dynasty](/wiki/Ilyas_Shahi_dynasty "Ilyas Shahi dynasty") highly acknowledged [Muslim scholarship](/wiki/Ulama "Ulama"). [Usman Serajuddin](/wiki/Usman_Serajuddin "Usman Serajuddin"), also known as *Akhi Siraj Bengali*, was a native of [Gaur](/wiki/Gau%E1%B8%8Da_(city) "Gauḍa (city)") in western Bengal and became the Sultanate's court scholar during Ilyas Shah's reign. The sovereign Sunni Muslim nation-state also enabled the language of the Bengali people to gain patronage and support, contrary to previous states which exclusively favoured [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"), [Pali](/wiki/Pali "Pali") and [Persian](/wiki/Farsi "Farsi"). The converted Sultan [Jalaluddin Muhammad Shah](/wiki/Jalaluddin_Muhammad_Shah "Jalaluddin Muhammad Shah") funded the construction of Islamic seminaries as far as [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca") and [Madina](/wiki/Madina "Madina") in the Middle East. The people of [Arabia](/wiki/Arabia "Arabia") came to know these institutions as [al-Madaris al-Bangaliyyah](/wiki/Banjaliyah_Madrasah "Banjaliyah Madrasah") (*Bengali madrasas*).

[Moroccan](/wiki/Moroccans "Moroccans") traveler [Ibn Battuta](/wiki/Ibn_Battuta "Ibn Battuta")'s diary is one of the best known accounts of the prelude to the Bengal Sultanate. Ibn Battuta visited Bengal during the reign of Sultan [Fakhruddin Mubarak Shah](/wiki/Fakhruddin_Mubarak_Shah "Fakhruddin Mubarak Shah"), a rebel governor of the Delhi Sultanate who established a city state in [Sonargaon](/wiki/Sonargaon "Sonargaon"). At the time, Bengal was divided into the three city states of Sonargaon, [Satgaon](/wiki/Satgaon "Satgaon") and [Lakhnauti](/wiki/Gauda_(city) "Gauda (city)"). In 1352, the three city states were united by Ilyas Shah into a single, unitary, independent Bengal Sultanate. The creation of the Bengal Sultanate sparked several [Bengal-Delhi Wars](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate-Delhi_Sultanate_War "Bengal Sultanate-Delhi Sultanate War"), which resulted in Delhi recognizing Bengal's independence. The [Ilyas Shahi dynasty](/wiki/Ilyas_Shahi_dynasty "Ilyas Shahi dynasty") consolidated Bengali statehood, the economy and diplomatic relations. A network of Mint Towns – provincial capitals which produced the Sultan's sovereign currency called the *[taka](/wiki/History_of_the_taka "History of the taka")* – was established across Bengal. The Bengali state followed the Persian model of statecraft. Muslims from other parts of the world were imported for military, bureaucratic and household services. These immigrants included Turks from northern India who were originally recruited in Central Asia; as well as Abyssinians imported via East Africa into the Bengali port of Chittagong. A highly commercialized and monetized economy evolved. [Islamic architecture](/wiki/Islamic_architecture "Islamic architecture") was introduced on a major scale. A huge mosque called the [Adina Mosque](/wiki/Adina_Mosque "Adina Mosque") was built following the design of the [Great Mosque of Damascus](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Damascus "Great Mosque of Damascus"). A distinct Bengali Muslim architectural style developed, with [terracotta](/wiki/Terracotta "Terracotta") and [stone](/wiki/Stone "Stone") buildings showing a fusion of Persian and Bengali elements. Mosques included two categories, including multi-domed rectangular structures and single-domed square structures. A distinct style of Bengali *[mihrabs](/wiki/Mihrab "Mihrab")*, *[minbars](/wiki/Minbar "Minbar")*, terracotta [arabesque](/wiki/Arabesque "Arabesque"), and *[do-chala](/wiki/Do-chala "Do-chala")* roofs developed; this influence also spread to other regions.

The Bengal Sultanate was ruled by five dynastic periods, with each period have a particular ethnic identity. The Ilyas Shahi dynasty was of [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_peoples "Turkic peoples") origin. It was replaced by the Bengali-origin dynasty of [Jalaluddin Muhammad Shah](/wiki/Jalaluddin_Muhammad_Shah "Jalaluddin Muhammad Shah") and [Shamsuddin Ahmad Shah](/wiki/Shamsuddin_Ahmad_Shah "Shamsuddin Ahmad Shah") for a few decades before being restored. In the 1490s, a series of [Abyssinian](/wiki/Habesha_people "Habesha people") generals took turns in becoming the Sultan of Bengal. They were succeeded by the [Hussain Shahi dynasty](/wiki/Hussain_Shahi_dynasty "Hussain Shahi dynasty") which was of [Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") origin. They were in turn replaced by the [Pashtun](/wiki/Pashtun "Pashtun") rulers of the [Suri dynasty](/wiki/Suri_Empire "Suri Empire"), who first acted as regional governors before restoring Bengali independence. The last dynasty, the [Karrani dynasty](/wiki/Karrani_dynasty "Karrani dynasty"), was also of Pashtun origin. The sultanate period saw a flourishing of Islamic scholarship and the development of [Bengali literature](/wiki/Bengali_literature "Bengali literature"). Scholars, writers and poets of sultanate-era Bengal included [Usman Serajuddin](/wiki/Usman_Serajuddin "Usman Serajuddin"), [Alaul Haq](/wiki/Alaul_Haq "Alaul Haq"), [Sheikh Nur Qutb Alam](/wiki/Nur_Qutb_Alam "Nur Qutb Alam"), [Alaol](/wiki/Alaol "Alaol"), [Shah Muhammad Sagir](/wiki/Shah_Muhammad_Sagir "Shah Muhammad Sagir"), [Abdul Hakim](/wiki/Abdul_Hakim_(poet) "Abdul Hakim (poet)"), [Syed Sultan](/wiki/Syed_Sultan "Syed Sultan"), Qadi Ruknu'd-Din Abu Hamid Muhammad bin Muhammad al-'Amidi, Abu Tawwama, [Syed Ibrahim Danishmand](/wiki/Ibrahim_Danishmand "Ibrahim Danishmand"), Syed Arif Billah Muhammad Kamel and Syed Muhammad Yusuf among others. Bengal's tradition of [Persian prose](/wiki/Persian_literature "Persian literature") was acknowledged by [Hafez](/wiki/Hafez "Hafez"). The *[Dobhashi](/wiki/Dobhashi "Dobhashi")* tradition saw [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") transliteration of [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") and Persian words in [Bengali script](/wiki/Bengali_script "Bengali script") to illustrate Islamic epics and stories.

During the independent sultanate period, Bengal forged strong diplomatic relations with empires outside the subcontinent. The most notable of these relationships was with [Ming China](/wiki/Ming_China "Ming China") and its emperor [Yongle](/wiki/Yongle "Yongle"). At least a dozen embassies were exchanged between China and Bengal. The Sultan of Bengal even gifted an East African giraffe to the Emperor of China as a tribute to China-Bengal relations. The Chinese Muslim admiral [Zheng He](/wiki/Zheng_He "Zheng He") visited Bengal as an envoy of the Emperor of China. Bengali ships transported the embassies of [Sumatra](/wiki/Aceh_Sultanate "Aceh Sultanate"), [Brunei](/wiki/Bruneian_Empire "Bruneian Empire") and [Malacca](/wiki/Malacca_Sultanate "Malacca Sultanate") to the port of [Canton](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou"). China and the [Timurid](/wiki/Timurid_dynasty "Timurid dynasty") ruler of [Herat](/wiki/Herat "Herat") mediated an end to the [Bengal Sultanate-Jaunpur Sultanate War](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate-Jaunpur_Sultanate_War "Bengal Sultanate-Jaunpur Sultanate War").[[20]](#cite_note-20) The Sultan of Bengal also acknowledged the nominal authority of the [Abbasid](/wiki/Abbasid "Abbasid") caliph in [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo"). The [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India") was the first European state entity to establish relations with the Bengal Sultanate. The Bengal Sultan permitted the opening of the [Portuguese settlement in Chittagong](/wiki/Portuguese_settlement_in_Chittagong "Portuguese settlement in Chittagong").

During the [Sultanate period](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Bengal "Sultanate of Bengal"), a [syncretic belief](/wiki/Syncretism#Religious_syncretism "Syncretism") system arose due to mass conversions.[[21]](#cite_note-:2-21) As a result, the Islamic concept of *[tawhid](/wiki/Tawhid "Tawhid")* (the oneness of God) was diluted into the veneration of saints or *[pirs](/wiki/Pir_(Sufism) "Pir (Sufism)")*. Deities such as [Shitala](/wiki/Shitala "Shitala") (goddess of [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox")), [Olabibi](/wiki/Oladevi "Oladevi") (goddess of [cholera](/wiki/Cholera "Cholera")) and [Manasa](/wiki/Manasa "Manasa") (goddess of snakes) became venerated as *pirs*.[[22]](#cite_note-:1-22)

### Under Mughal Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Under Mughal Empire")]

Main articles: [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") and [Bengal Subah](/wiki/Bengal_Subah "Bengal Subah")

In pre-Mughal times, there is less evidence for widespread adoption of Islam in what is now Bangladesh. What mention of Muslims there was usually in reference to an urban elite. Ibn Battuta met with Shah Jalal in Sylhet and noted the inhabitants of the plains were still Hindu. In 1591, Venetian traveller Cesare Federici mentioned [Sondwip](/wiki/Sandwip "Sandwip") near Chittagong as having an entirely Muslim population. The seventeenth century European travellers generally understood Islam as being implanted after the Mughal conquest.[[23]](#cite_note-:7-23)

During the Mughal Empire, much of the region of what is now East Bengal was still heavily forested, but highly fertile. The Mughals incentivised the bringing of this land under cultivation, and so peasants were incentivised to bring the land under cultivation. These peasants were primarily led by Muslim leaders and so Islam became the main religion in the delta. Most of the Zamindars in the modern Barisal division, for instance, were upper caste Hindus who subcontracted actual jungle clearance work to a Muslim pir. In other instances, pirs themselves would organise the locals to clear the jungle and then contact the Mughals to gain legitimacy. In other instances, such as the densely forested interior of Chittagong, Muslims came from indigenous tribals who never followed Hindu rituals.[[23]](#cite_note-:7-23)

### In British India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: In British India")]

Main articles: [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") and [Bengal Presidency](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency")

The [British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company") was given the right to collect revenue from Bengal and Bihar by the [Treaty of Allahabad](/wiki/Treaty_of_Allahabad "Treaty of Allahabad") after defeating the combined armies of Nawab [Mir Qasim](/wiki/Mir_Qasim "Mir Qasim") of Bengal, [Nawab of Awadh](/wiki/Nawab_of_Awadh "Nawab of Awadh"), and Mughal emperor at the [Battle of Buxar](/wiki/Battle_of_Buxar "Battle of Buxar"). They annexed Bengal in 1793 after abolishing local rule (Nizamat). The British looted the Bengal treasury, appropriating wealth valued at US$40 billion in modern-day prices.[[24]](#cite_note-DailyStar-24) Due to high colonial taxation, Bengali commerce shrank by 50% within 40 years, while at the same time British imports flooded the market. Spinners and weavers starved during [famines](/wiki/Famine "Famine") and Bengal's once industrious cities became impoverished. The East India Company forced [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium") and [indigo](/wiki/Indigo "Indigo") cultivation and the [permanent settlement](/wiki/Permanent_settlement "Permanent settlement") dismantled centuries of joint Muslim-Hindu political, military and feudal cooperation.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

The [Bengal Presidency](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency") was established in 1765. Rural eastern Bengal witnessed the earliest rebellions against British rule, including the [Faraizi movement](/wiki/Faraizi_movement "Faraizi movement") led by [Haji Shariatullah](/wiki/Haji_Shariatullah "Haji Shariatullah") and the activities of [Titumir](/wiki/Titumir "Titumir") and [Karamat Ali Jaunpuri](/wiki/Karamat_Ali_Jaunpuri "Karamat Ali Jaunpuri"). The [mutiny of 1857](/wiki/Mutiny_of_1857 "Mutiny of 1857") engulfed much of northern India and Bengal, including in [Dhaka](/wiki/Dhaka "Dhaka") and [Chittagong](/wiki/Chittagong "Chittagong").[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) Following the end of the mutiny, the British Government took direct control of Bengal from the East India Company and instituted the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"). The influence of [Christian missionaries](/wiki/Christian_missionaries "Christian missionaries") increased during this period. To counter this trend, Reazuddin Ahmad Mashadi, Muhammad Reazuddin Ahmad[[28]](#cite_note-28) of the *[Sudhakar](/wiki/The_Sudhakar "The Sudhakar")* newspaper and [Munshi Mohammad Meherullah](/wiki/Munshi_Mohammad_Meherullah "Munshi Mohammad Meherullah") played prominent roles.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The colonial capital [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta"), where [Bengali Muslims](/wiki/Bengali_Muslims "Bengali Muslims") formed the second largest community, became the second largest city in the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") after London. The late 19th and early 20th-century Indian Renaissance brought dramatic social and political change. The introduction of Western law, government and education introduced modern [enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") values which created a new politically conscious middle class and a new generation of leaders in science, politics and the arts. The [First Partition of Bengal](/wiki/Partition_of_Bengal_(1905) "Partition of Bengal (1905)") incubated the broader anti-colonial struggle and in 1906 the [All India Muslim League](/wiki/All_India_Muslim_League "All India Muslim League") was formed during the [Muhammadan Education Conference](/wiki/All_India_Muhammadan_Educational_Conference "All India Muhammadan Educational Conference") in Dhaka. During this period a Muslim middle class emerged[[30]](#cite_note-:0-30) and the [University of Dhaka](/wiki/University_of_Dhaka "University of Dhaka") played a role at the beginning of the emancipation of Bengali Muslim society, which was also marked by the emergence progressive groups like the [Freedom of Intellect Movement](/wiki/Freedom_of_Intellect_Movement "Freedom of Intellect Movement") and the Muslim Literary Society.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Bengali Muslims were at the forefront of the [Indian Independence Movement](/wiki/Indian_Independence_Movement "Indian Independence Movement"), including the [Pakistan Movement](/wiki/Pakistan_Movement "Pakistan Movement").

### Bangladesh War of Independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Bangladesh War of Independence")]

Main article: [Bangladesh Liberation War](/wiki/Bangladesh_Liberation_War "Bangladesh Liberation War")

See also: [History of East Pakistan](/wiki/History_of_East_Pakistan "History of East Pakistan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Mufti_Mahmud_%26_Sheikh_Mujibur_Rahman.jpg/250px-Mufti_Mahmud_%26_Sheikh_Mujibur_Rahman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mufti_Mahmud_%26_Sheikh_Mujibur_Rahman.jpg)

President [Sheikh Mujibur Rahman](/wiki/Sheikh_Mujibur_Rahman "Sheikh Mujibur Rahman") with [Mufti Mahmud](/wiki/Mufti_Mahmud "Mufti Mahmud").

Islamic sentiments powered the definition of nationhood in the 1940s, when [Bengali](/wiki/Bengalis "Bengalis") people united with Muslims in other parts of the subcontinent to form Pakistan. In the 70s, the society they then envisioned was based on principles such as socialism, nationalism and democracy. While Islam was still a part of faith and culture, it was no longer the only factor that formed national identity.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Bangladesh was established as a constitutionally secular state[[32]](#cite_note-32) and the Bangladeshi constitution enshrined secular, socialist and democratic principles.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Sunni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Sunni")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Shah_Jalal_Mazar_at_Sylhet.JPG/250px-Shah_Jalal_Mazar_at_Sylhet.JPG)](/wiki/File:Shah_Jalal_Mazar_at_Sylhet.JPG)

Shah Jalal Mazar at Sylhet

The majority of the [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") in Bangladesh are *[Sunni](/wiki/Sunni "Sunni")*, who mainly follow the *[Hanafi](/wiki/Hanafi "Hanafi")* school of Islamic jurisprudence and the *[Maturidi](/wiki/Maturidi "Maturidi")* school of theology.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Small minorities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Small minorities")]

Further information: [Shia Islam in Bangladesh](/wiki/Shia_Islam_in_Bangladesh "Shia Islam in Bangladesh") and [Ahmadiyya in Bangladesh](/wiki/Ahmadiyya_in_Bangladesh "Ahmadiyya in Bangladesh")

There are also few [Shi'a](/wiki/Shi%27a_Islam "Shi'a Islam") Muslims, particularly belonging to the [Bihari](/wiki/Bihari_Muslims "Bihari Muslims") community. The Shi'a observance commemorating the martyrdom of [Ali](/wiki/Ali "Ali")'s sons, [Hasan](/wiki/Hasan_ibn_Ali "Hasan ibn Ali") and [Husayn](/wiki/Husayn_ibn_Ali "Husayn ibn Ali"), are still widely observed by the nation's Sunnis,[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) even though there are small numbers of Shi'as. Among the Shias, the [Dawoodi Bohra](/wiki/Dawoodi_Bohra "Dawoodi Bohra") community is concentrated in Chittagong.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Those who reject the authority of [hadith](/wiki/Hadith "Hadith"), known as [Quranists](/wiki/Quranism "Quranism"), are present in Bangladesh, though having not expressed publicly but are active virtually due to fear of gruesome persecution considering the present political situation. The [Ahmadiyya](/wiki/Ahmadiyya "Ahmadiyya") community, which is widely considered to be non-Muslim by mainstream Muslim leaders, is estimated to be around 100,000, the community has faced discrimination because of their beliefs and have been persecuted in some areas.[[38]](#cite_note-state-38) There is a very small community of Bangladeshis whom are adherents to the [Mahdavia](/wiki/Mahdavia "Mahdavia") creed.[[39]](#cite_note-39) There are some people who do not identify themselves with any sect and just call themselves Muslims.

Estimation shows that over 1 million [Rohingya](/wiki/Rohingya "Rohingya") Muslim and Hindu refugees live in Bangladesh who have came here during the period of (2016–17) crisis.[[47]](#cite_note-47) On 28 September 2018, at the 73rd United Nations General Assembly, Bangladeshi Prime Minister Sheikh Hasina said there are 1.1-1.3 million Rohingya refugees now in Bangladesh.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

According to the Pew research center, Muslim population of Bangladesh will reach 218.5-237.5 million by the year 2050, and will constitute overwhelming 95% of the country's population, thus making the country 4th largest Muslim populated around that time.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-pewforum.org-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/201_Dome_Mosque_02.jpg/250px-201_Dome_Mosque_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:201_Dome_Mosque_02.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Sura_Mosque_Terakota_5.jpg/250px-Sura_Mosque_Terakota_5.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sura_Mosque_Terakota_5.jpg)

[Terracotta](/wiki/Terracotta "Terracotta") art of [Sura Mosque](/wiki/Sura_Mosque "Sura Mosque")

The architecture of the [Bengal Sultanate](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate") saw a distinct style of domed mosques with complex niche pillars that had no minarets. [Ivory](/wiki/Ivory "Ivory"), [pottery](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery") and [brass](/wiki/Brass "Brass") were also widely used in [Bengali art](/wiki/Bangladeshi_art "Bangladeshi art"). Bangladeshi village housing is noted as the origin of the [bungalow](/wiki/Bungalow "Bungalow"). The [Bengal roofs](/wiki/Bengal_roofs "Bengal roofs"), associated by [mughal architecture](/wiki/Mughal_architecture "Mughal architecture") can be traced back to rural Bangladesh's architecture. Modernist terracotta architecture in South Asia is popularized by architects like [Muzharul Islam](/wiki/Muzharul_Islam "Muzharul Islam").[[53]](#cite_note-53)

#### Textile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Textile")]

Further information: [Textile industry of Bangladesh](/wiki/Textile_industry_of_Bangladesh "Textile industry of Bangladesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Making_of_Jamdani-9.jpg/250px-Making_of_Jamdani-9.jpg)](/wiki/File:Making_of_Jamdani-9.jpg)

[Jamdani](/wiki/Jamdani "Jamdani") Weaving

[Bengal](/wiki/Bengal_Subah "Bengal Subah") emerged as the foremost muslin exporter in the world, with [Dhaka](/wiki/Dhaka "Dhaka") as capital of the worldwide muslin trade.[[56]](#cite_note-Eaton1996-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) In the latter half of the 18th century, muslin weaving ceased in Bengal due to cheap fabrics from England and oppression by the colonialists.
[Mughal Bengal](/wiki/Mughal_Bengal "Mughal Bengal") was a major silk exporter. The Bengali silk industry declined after the growth of Japanese silk production. [Rajshahi silk](/wiki/Rajshahi_silk "Rajshahi silk") continues to be produced in northern Bangladesh.
[Mughal Bengal](/wiki/Mughal_Bengal "Mughal Bengal")'s most celebrated artistic tradition was the weaving of [Jamdani](/wiki/Jamdani "Jamdani") [motifs](/wiki/Motif_(textile_arts) "Motif (textile arts)") on fine muslin, which is now classified by UNESCO as an [intangible cultural heritage](/wiki/Intangible_cultural_heritage "Intangible cultural heritage"). Jamdani motifs were similar to Iranian textile art (buta motifs) and Western textile art ([paisley](/wiki/Paisley_(design) "Paisley (design)")). The Jamdani weavers in Dhaka received imperial patronage.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Modern Bangladesh is one of the world's [largest textile producers](/wiki/Bangladesh_textile_industry "Bangladesh textile industry"), with a large cotton based [ready made garments industry](/wiki/Bangladeshi_RMG_Sector "Bangladeshi RMG Sector").

#### Theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Theatre")]

Further information: [Theatre in Bangladesh](/wiki/Theatre_in_Bangladesh "Theatre in Bangladesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Jatra_theatre.jpg/250px-Jatra_theatre.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jatra_theatre.jpg)

[Jatra](/wiki/Jatra "Jatra") performance

The earliest extant literary evidence of the existence of the form in Bengal is a couplet in [Yusuf-Zulekha](/wiki/Yusuf-Zulekha "Yusuf-Zulekha") written by the medieval Bengali poet, [Shah Muhammad Sagir](/wiki/Shah_Muhammad_Sagir "Shah Muhammad Sagir"). By the 18th century, scroll-painting performances gained popularity even among the Muslims, as evinced by Gazir Pat (scroll-painting performances eulogising Pir Gazi), which can still be seen in Bangladesh today.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

#### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Music")]

Further information: [Music of Bangladesh](/wiki/Music_of_Bangladesh "Music of Bangladesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Baul_performance_at_Baul_Fakiri_Mela.jpg/250px-Baul_performance_at_Baul_Fakiri_Mela.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baul_performance_at_Baul_Fakiri_Mela.jpg)

[Baul](/wiki/Baul "Baul") is philosophy and music

A notable feature of Bengali Muslim music is the syncretic [Baul](/wiki/Baul "Baul") tradition, influenced by Sufism. The most prominent practitioner was [Lalon Shah](/wiki/Lalon_Shah "Lalon Shah"). Baul music of Bangladesh is included in the [UNESCO Masterpieces of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity](/wiki/UNESCO_Masterpieces_of_the_Oral_and_Intangible_Heritage_of_Humanity "UNESCO Masterpieces of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity"). [Alauddin Khan](/wiki/Alauddin_Khan "Alauddin Khan"), [Ali Akbar Khan](/wiki/Ali_Akbar_Khan "Ali Akbar Khan"), and [Gul Mohammad Khan](/wiki/Gul_Mohammad_Khan "Gul Mohammad Khan") were notable Muslim exponents of [Hindustani classical music](/wiki/Hindustani_classical_music "Hindustani classical music") from [East Bengal](/wiki/East_Bengal "East Bengal"). Artists like [Runa Laila](/wiki/Runa_Laila "Runa Laila") and [Shahnaz Rahmatullah](/wiki/Shahnaz_Rahmatullah "Shahnaz Rahmatullah") from [East Pakistan](/wiki/East_Pakistan "East Pakistan"), became widely acclaimed for their musical talents across South Asia in the field of modern music. [Nazrul Sangeet](/wiki/Nazrul_Sangeet "Nazrul Sangeet") is the collection of 4,000 songs and [ghazals](/wiki/Ghazals "Ghazals") written by the national poet, [Nazrul Islam](/wiki/Kazi_Nazrul_Islam "Kazi Nazrul Islam").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Eid_Prayers_at_Barashalghar%2C_Debidwar%2C_Comilla.jpg/250px-Eid_Prayers_at_Barashalghar%2C_Debidwar%2C_Comilla.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eid_Prayers_at_Barashalghar,_Debidwar,_Comilla.jpg)

Muslim males can be seen attending [Khutbah](/wiki/Khutbah "Khutbah") as part of the [Eid-ul-Adha](/wiki/Eid-ul-Adha "Eid-ul-Adha") [prayers](/wiki/Salat "Salat"). Photo taken at Barashalghar union of [Comilla's](/wiki/Comilla_District "Comilla District") [Debidwar upazila](/wiki/Debidwar_Upazila "Debidwar Upazila").

Although Islam played a significant role in the life and culture of the people, religion did not dominate national politics because Islam was not the central component of national identity.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) When in June 1988 an "Islamic way of life" was proclaimed for Bangladesh by constitutional amendment, very little attention was paid outside the intellectual class to the meaning and impact of such an important national commitment.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) However, most observers believed that the declaration of Islam as the state religion might have a significant impact on national life.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) Aside from arousing the suspicion of the non-Islamic minorities, it could accelerate the proliferation of religious parties at both the national and the local levels, thereby exacerbating tension and conflict between secular and religious politicians.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) Unrest of this nature was reported on some college campuses soon after the amendment was promulgated.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36)

### Legal issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Legal issues")]

In Bangladesh, where a modified Anglo-Indian civil and criminal legal system operates, there are no official sharia courts.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) Most Muslim marriages, however, are presided over by the *qazi*, a traditional Muslim judge whose advice is also sought on matters of personal law, such as inheritance, divorce, and [the administration of religious endowments](/wiki/Waqf "Waqf").[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36)

The inheritance rights of Muslim in Bangladesh are governed by The Muslim Personal Law ([Shariat](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia")) Application Act (1937)[[60]](#cite_note-bdlaws.minlaw.gov.bd-60) and The Muslim Family Laws Ordinance (1961).[[61]](#cite_note-61) Article 2 of The Muslim Personal Law Application Act provides that questions related to succession and inheritance are governed by Muslim Personal Law (Shariat).[[60]](#cite_note-bdlaws.minlaw.gov.bd-60)[[62]](#cite_note-62)
Article 2 proclaims: "any custom or usage to the contrary, in all questions (save questions relating to agricultural land) regarding intestate succession, special property of females, including personal property inherited or obtained under contract or gift or any other provision of Personal Law, marriage, dissolution of marriage, including [talaq](/wiki/Divorce_in_Islam "Divorce in Islam"), ila, [zihar](/wiki/Zihar "Zihar"), lian, [khula](/wiki/Khul%27 "Khul'") and mubaraat, maintenance, dower, guardianship, gifts, trusts and trust properties, and [waqfs](/wiki/Waqf "Waqf") (other than charities and charitable institutions and charitable and religious endowments) the rule of decision in cases where the parties are Muslims shall be the Muslim Personal Law (Shariat)."[[60]](#cite_note-bdlaws.minlaw.gov.bd-60)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Political issues")]

See also: [Islamization in Bangladesh](/wiki/Islamization_in_Bangladesh "Islamization in Bangladesh")

Post-1971 regimes sought to increase the role of the government in the religious life of the people.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) The Ministry of Religious Affairs provided support, financial assistance, and endowments to religious institutions, including mosques and community prayer grounds ([Idgahs](/wiki/Eidgah "Eidgah")).[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) The organization of [Hajj](/wiki/Hajj "Hajj") also came under the auspices of the ministry because of limits on the number of pilgrims admitted by the government of [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") and the restrictive foreign exchange regulations of the government of Bangladesh.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) The ministry also directed the policy and the program of the [Islamic Foundation Bangladesh](/wiki/Islamic_Foundation_Bangladesh "Islamic Foundation Bangladesh"), which was responsible for organizing and supporting research and publications on Islamic subjects.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) The foundation also maintains the [Baitul Mukarram](/wiki/Baitul_Mukarram_National_Mosque "Baitul Mukarram National Mosque") (National Mosque), and organized the training of imams.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) Some 18,000 imams were scheduled for training once the government completed establishment of a national network of Islamic cultural centers and mosque libraries.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) Under the patronage of the Islamic Foundation, an encyclopedia of Islam in the Bengali language was being compiled in the late 1980s.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36)

Another step toward further government involvement in religious life was taken in 1984 when the semi-official [Zakat](/wiki/Zakat "Zakat") Fund Committee was established under the chairmanship of the president of Bangladesh.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) The committee solicited annual zakat contributions on a voluntary basis.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) The revenue so generated was to be spent on orphanages, schools, children's hospitals, and other charitable institutions and projects.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) Commercial banks and other financial institutions were encouraged to contribute to the fund.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) Through these measures the government sought closer ties with religious establishments within the country and with Islamic countries such as Saudi Arabia and Pakistan.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36)

## Leaders and organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Leaders and organizations")]

The members of the Ulama include [Mawlānā](/wiki/Mawl%C4%81n%C4%81 "Mawlānā"), [Imams](/wiki/Imam "Imam"), [Ulama](/wiki/Ulama "Ulama") and [Muftis](/wiki/Mufti "Mufti").[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) The first two titles are accorded to those who have received special training in Islamic theology and law.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) A maulvi has pursued higher studies in a [madrassa](/wiki/Madrassa "Madrassa"), a school of religious education attached to a mosque. Additional study on the graduate level leads to the title Mawlānā.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36)

### Educational institutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Educational institutions")]

The madrassas are also divided in two mainstreams; [Alia Madrasah](/wiki/Bangladesh_Madrasah_Education_Board "Bangladesh Madrasah Education Board") and [Qawmi Madrasah](/wiki/Qawmi_madrasa "Qawmi madrasa").

## Status of religious freedom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_Bangladesh&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Status of religious freedom")]

Further information: [Freedom of religion in Bangladesh](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion_in_Bangladesh "Freedom of religion in Bangladesh") and [Secularism in Bangladesh](/wiki/Secularism_in_Bangladesh "Secularism in Bangladesh")

See also: [Persecution of Ahmadiyya](/wiki/Persecution_of_Ahmadiyya "Persecution of Ahmadiyya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Saying_Juma_Namaz_%28Friday_prayer_for_Muslims%29%2C_Dhaka%2C_Bangladesh_NK.JPG/250px-Saying_Juma_Namaz_%28Friday_prayer_for_Muslims%29%2C_Dhaka%2C_Bangladesh_NK.JPG)](/wiki/File:Saying_Juma_Namaz_(Friday_prayer_for_Muslims),_Dhaka,_Bangladesh_NK.JPG)

Friday prayer for Muslims in Dhaka

The Constitution establishes Islam as the state religion but upholds the right to practice—subject to law, public order, and morality—the religion of one's choice.[[63]](#cite_note-63) The Government generally respects this provision in practice. The Government (2001–2006) led by an alliance of four parties [Bangladesh Nationalist Party](/wiki/Bangladesh_Nationalist_Party "Bangladesh Nationalist Party"), [Jamaat-e-Islami Bangladesh](/wiki/Jamaat-e-Islami_Bangladesh "Jamaat-e-Islami Bangladesh"), [Islami Oikya Jote](/wiki/Islami_Oikya_Jote "Islami Oikya Jote") and [Bangladesh Jatiyo Party](/wiki/Jatiya_Party_(Ershad) "Jatiya Party (Ershad)") banned [Ahmadiya](/wiki/Ahmadiyya "Ahmadiyya") literature by an executive order. The current interim government led by [Muhammad Yunus](/wiki/Muhammad_Yunus "Muhammad Yunus") also propagates secularism.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)
